flashcards.front: How many signal lines are needed for basic SPI communication?
flashcards.back: 4 lines: MOSI, MISO, SCK, and SS

flashcards.front: What is the function of the SS pin in SPI?
flashcards.back: Slave Select - used to select and activate a specific slave device
Master Out Slave In - data line from master to slave
Master In Slave Out - data line from slave to master
Serial Clock - generated by the master to synchronize data transfer

flashcards.front: What does CPOL stand for in SPI modes?
flashcards.back: Clock Polarity - determines the idle state of the clock signal

flashcards.front: What does CPHA stand for in SPI modes?
flashcards.back: Clock Phase - determines when data is sampled relative to clock edges

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of SPI over I2C?

flashcards.back: Data can be transmitted and received simultaneously

flashcards.front: Name two common applications of SPI
What is a disadvantage of SPI compared to I2C?
flashcards.back: Requires more I/O pins, especially with multiple slaves

flashcards.front: How are multiple slaves typically handled in SPI?
flashcards.back: Using separate Slave Select lines for each slave or daisy-chaining

flashcards.front: What is bit-banging in SPI context?
flashcards.back: Implementing SPI protocol in software when hardware SPI is unavailable

flashcards.front: Does SPI have built-in error checking?
flashcards.back: No, SPI does not have built-in error detection or acknowledgment
